elements.sent-2411-001: We prove that if $D$ is a digraph of maximum outdegree and indegree at least $k$, and minimum semidegree at least $k/2$ that contains no oriented $4$-cycles, then $D$ contains each oriented tree $T$ with~$k$ arcs.
elements.sent-2411-002: This can be slightly improved if $T$ is either antidirected or an arborescence.
elements.sent-2411-003: Any undirected graph $G$ of minimum degree $\delta(G)$ at least $k$ contains each $k$-edge tree: Indeed, we can just greedily embed the tree.
elements.sent-2411-004: Because of the $k$-edge stars, the bound on $\delta(G)$ cannot be made lower than $k$, unless some extra condition on $G$ is added.
elements.sent-2411-005: Clearly, one necessary condition is that $G$ has a vertex that can accommodate the highest degree vertex of $T$, in other words, $G$ has maximum degree $\Delta (G)\ge \Delta(T)$.
elements.sent-2411-006: This alone is not enough, which can be seen for instance by considering two copies of the clique on $\lfloor k/2\rfloor-1$ vertices and adding a universal vertex.
elements.sent-2411-007: This graph does not contain any $k$-edge tree having a vertex with three roughly equal-sized components attached to it.
elements.sent-2411-008: Therefore, we should either change the bounds on $\Delta(G)$ and/or $\delta(G)$ (for a discussion of this option see Section~\ref{sec:otherdegree ), or add more conditions on $G$ apart from $\delta (G)\ge k/2$ and $\Delta (G)\ge \Delta(T)$.}
elements.sent-2411-009: A natural candidate for such an additional condition is to ask for some kind of expansion of~$G$.
elements.sent-2411-010: In particular, assuming that $G$ has large girth should be helpful for finding trees.
elements.sent-2411-011: Brandt and Dobson~\cite{erdos-sos-girth5 showed that every graph of girth at least five, with $\delta(G)\ge k/2$ and with $\Delta(G)\ge \Delta (T)$ contains each $k$-edge tree.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Generalising this result, Sacl\'e and Wo\'zniak~\cite{sacle proved\footnote{The main result of~\cite{sacle is that every graph $G$ that has more than $\frac{k-1 2|V(G)|$ edges and no $C_4$-cycles contains each $k$-edge tree.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The result we are referring to here is alluded to in Section~4 of~\cite{sacle .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Extending ideas from~\cite{sacle , we prove an analogous result for digraphs.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The minimum semidegree $\delta^0(D)$ of a digraph $D$ is the minimum of the indegrees and outdegrees of the vertices of $D$, and its {\it maximum total degree $\Delta^{tot (D)$ is the maximum degree of the underlying graph.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: We write $\Delta^\pm(D)$ for the smallest $m$ such that $D$ has a vertex of outdegree at least~$m$ and a vertex of indegree at least $m$ (these vertices may coincide).
elements.sent-2411-017: Our main result is the following.
elements.sent-2411-018: If $\Delta^\pm(D)> \Delta^{tot (T)$ then $D$ contains $T$ as a subgraph.}
elements.sent-2411-019: See Section~\ref{forbid for a discussion on forbidding a smaller set of oriented $4$-cycles, or a a different family of digraphs.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Further, note that $ \Delta^{tot (T)$ equals $k$ only if $T$ is a star, and in that case $T$ embeds in $D$ as long as $\Delta^\pm(D)\ge k$ and $\delta^0(D)\ge k/2$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Hence Theorem~\ref{thm:oriented has the following corollary, which is the result mentioned in the abstract.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Then $D$ contains each oriented tree on $k$ arcs as a subgraph.
elements.sent-2411-023: If the tree we are looking for is antidirected, we can replace the minimum semidegree with the minimum pseudo-semidegree $\bar\delta^0(D)$ of $D$: this is $0$ if $D$ has no arcs, and otherwise is defined as the largest integer $d$ such that no vertex $v$ in $D$ has positive indegree $<d$ or positive outdegree $<d$.
elements.sent-2411-024: Moreover, we allow the host $D$ to have directed $4$-cycles.
elements.sent-2411-025: This is a special case of a conjecture on antidirected trees in digraphs of high arc density from~\cite{ahlrt .}
elements.sent-2411-027: If $T$ is an out-arborescence we only need to bound the outdegree of $D$, and for oriented $D$, the bound on the minimum semidegree can be lower than in Theorem~\ref{thm:oriented .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Let $D$ be a digraph having no oriented $4$-cycles with $\Delta^+(D)> \max\{\Delta^+(T), k/2\}$ and $\delta^+(D)\ge k/2-\lambda$, where $\lambda=1$ if $D$ is an oriented graph and $\lambda=0$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-030: The remainder of this paper is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-031: After going through some notation in Section~\ref{sec:notation , we give the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:oriented in Section~\ref{sec:oriented .}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: In Sections~\ref{sec:antidirected ~and~\ref{sec:arborescences we show how the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:oriented can be modified to prove Theorems~\ref{thm:antidirected ~and~\ref{thm:arborescences1 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Some open questions and the implication of Theorem~\ref{thm:antidirected for the conjecture from~\cite{ahlrt are discussed in Section~\ref{conclusion .}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: For a vertex $a$ of a digraph $D$, the out-neighbourhood (in-neighbourhood; total neighbourhood) of $a$, denoted by $N^+(a)$ ($N^-(a)$; $N(a)$) is the set of out-neighbours (in-neighbours; out- and in-neighbours) of $a$.
elements.sent-2411-035: The outdegree, indegree and total degree of $a$ are $\deg^+(a):=|N^+(a)|$, $\deg^-(a):=|N^-(a)|$ and $\deg(a):=|N(a)|$.
elements.sent-2411-036: The minimum outdegree (minimum indegree; minimum total degree) of $D$ is $\delta^+(D):=\min_{a\in D}\{\deg^+(a)\}$ ($\delta^-(D):=\min_{a\in D}\{\deg^-(a)\}$; $\delta(D):=\min_{a\in D}\{\deg(a)\}$).
elements.sent-2411-037: The maximum outdegree (maximum indegree; maximum total degree) of $D$ are $\Delta^+(D):=\max_{a\in D}\{\deg^+(a)\}$ ($\Delta^-(D):=\max_{a\in D}\{\deg^-(a)\}$; $\Delta(D):=\max_{a\in D}\{\deg(a)\}$).
elements.sent-2411-038: Call $D$ {\it antidirected if each of its vertices has indegree $0$ or outdegree $0$.}
elements.sent-2411-039: A non-isolated vertex~$a$ of a digraph $D$ is an out-vertex (in-vertex) if $\deg^-(a)=0$ ($\deg^+(a)=0$).
elements.sent-2411-040: The distance $\dist(a,b)$ of vertices $a,b\in V(D)$ is the length of a shortest path of any orientation that joins them.
elements.sent-2411-041: The diameter of $D$ is the maximum distance between any pair of its vertices.
elements.sent-2411-042: If $a$ and $b$ are vertices of an oriented tree, we denote by $P_{ab}$ the unique path joining them.
elements.sent-2411-043: An out-arborescence is an oriented rooted tree in which all edges point away from the root.
elements.sent-2411-044: Given an oriented tree $T$, a vertex $u\in V(T)$ is called a penultimate vertex if it is not a leaf of $T$ and all its neighbours, except at most one, are leaves.
elements.sent-2411-045: Given an oriented tree $T$ and a digraph $D$, an {\it embedding of $T$ into $D$ is an injective function $f: V(T) \to V(D)$ preserving adjacencies.}
elements.sent-2411-046: If such an embedding exists, we say that $T$ embeds in $D$, or $T$ is a subgraph of $D$.
elements.sent-2411-047: For a subset $X\subseteq V(T)$ and an embedding $f$ from $T$ to $D$, we write $f(X)$ to denote the image of $X$ in $D$.
elements.sent-2411-048: For convenience, let us define two classes of digraphs, \C and \Ccc \, digraphs.
elements.sent-2411-049: A digraph $D$ is $\mathcal{C_4$-free if it does not contain any orientation of a $4$-cycle as a subgraph, and it is $\mathcal{C^*_4$-free if any $4$-cycle in $D$ is a directed cycle.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: In order to be able to easily refer to this proof later, when we prove Theorem~\ref{thm:antidirected , we will frequently use the weaker property that $D$ is \Ccc \ (instead of using the property that it is \Cc).}
elements.sent-2411-051: Let $t$ be a vertex of maximum total degree of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Without loss of generality we assume that $\deg^+(t)\ge \deg^-(t)$ (the other case is analogous).
elements.sent-2411-053: Let $T_1$ be a maximal subtree of $T$ having diameter at most four that contains $t$ and $N(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-054: We will show that \begin{equation}\label{claim:T_1} \text{$T_1$ embeds in $D$.}\end{equation} For this, let $t'$ be a neighbour of $t$, and note that $\deg^{tot (t')\le \lfloor (k+1)/2\rfloor$, with equality if $T$ is a double-star.}

elements.sent-2411-168: Specifically, we will verify the following. \begin{equation}\label{eq:anti-second_claim} \begin{minipage}{0.8\textwidth} \textrm{ In the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:oriented}, whenever the tree $T$ is antidirected, it suffices to consider, for every vertex $v$ in $D$, either its out-neighbourhood (if $d^+(v)>0$) or its in-neighbourhood (if $d^-(v)>0$). } \end{minipage}\end{equation} When embedding the subtree $T_1$, we begin by embedding the double star with centers $t$ and $t'$.
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation}\label{eq:anti-second_claim} \begin{minipage}{0.8\textwidth} \textrm{ In the proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:oriented}, whenever the tree $T$ is antidirected, it suffices to consider, for every vertex $v$ in $D$, either its out-neighbourhood (if $d^+(v)>0$) or its in-neighbourhood (if $d^-(v)>0$). } \end{minipage}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-169: Without loss of generality, assume that $t$ is an out-vertex; otherwise interchange $+$ and $-$ in the following argument.

elements.sent-2411-171: Next, when embedding the neighbours of a non-leaf vertex $u$ of $T_1$ that has an already embedded neighbour $w$, it suffices to consider either $N^+(\td u)$ or $N^-(\td u)$, depending on whether $u$ is an out-vertex or an in-vertex.

elements.subsection-2411-001: Implications for a conjecture on antidirected trees
elements.sent-2411-227: In 2013, Addario-Berry, Havet, Linhares Sales, Reed and Thomassé~\cite{ahlrt proposed the following conjecture, which by previous work of Burr~\cite{burr would be optimal.}}
elements.sent-2411-228: This conjecture was confirmed in~\cite{ahlrt for stars and double-stars, and an asymptotic version for large bounded degree trees was proved in~\cite{camila .}}
elements.sent-2411-229: Very recently, the present authors established in~\cite{stein-trujillo two special cases of Conjecture~\ref{antitreeconj .}}
elements.sent-2411-230: First, it holds whenever the antidirected tree is a caterpillar.
elements.sent-2411-231: Second, it holds whenever the host digraph $D$ does not contain any of three specific orientations of $K_{2,\lceil k/12\rceil}$, namely the ones where each of the two vertices in the partition class of size two has either outdegree~$0$ or indegree $0$.
elements.sent-2411-232: Theorem~\ref{thm:antidirected in the present paper has the following corollary (for a sketch of a proof see below), which states that Conjecture~\ref{antitreeconj holds if all $4$-cycles of $D$ are directed (this includes the case that $D$ has no oriented $4$-cycles at all) and $\Delta^{tot (T)\le k/2$:.}}}
elements.sent-2411-233: Every digraph $D$ with more than $(k-1)|V(D)|$ arcs whose $4$-cycles are all directed contains each antidirected tree $T$ with $k$ arcs and $\Delta^{tot (T)\le k/2$.}
elements.sent-2411-234: Note that for $k\ge 13$, this corollary also follows from the results of~\cite{stein-trujillo , as then each of the forbidden orientations of $K_{2,\lceil k/12\rceil}$ contains a $4$-cycle that is not directed.}
elements.sent-2411-235: We deduce Corollary~\ref{coroanti from Theorem~\ref{thm:antidirected as follows.}}
elements.sent-2411-236: Given an antidirected tree $T$ with $k$ arcs and a digraph $D$ with more than $(k-1)|V(D)|$ arcs and whose only $4$-cycles are directed, we use Lemma~9 from~\cite{antipaths , which says that every $n$-vertex digraph with more than $(k-1)n$ arcs has a subdigraph of minimum pseudo-semidegree at least $k/2$.}
elements.sent-2411-237: Applying this lemma to $D$, we obtain a subdigraph $D'$ of~$D$ with $\bar\delta^0(D')\ge k/2$ whose only $4$-cycles are directed.
elements.sent-2411-238: Then Theorem~\ref{thm:antidirected provides an embedding of~$T$ in $D'$, and thus in $D$.}
elements.sent-2411-239: Let us now discuss alternatives to forbidding all oriented $4$-cycles.
elements.sent-2411-240: We first describe the situation for graphs.
elements.sent-2411-241: As we saw in the beginning of the introduction, a graph of minimum degree at least $k/2$ and maximum degree at least $k$ does not necessarily contain all trees on $k$ edges.
elements.sent-2411-242: However, this changes if we raise the bound on the minimum degree to almost $k$: In~\cite{havet , it was shown that there exists a $\gamma>0$ such that every graph $G$ with $\delta (G)\ge (1-\gamma)k$ and $\Delta(G)\ge k$ contains all trees on $k$ edges.}
elements.sent-2411-243: Havet, Reed, Stein and Wood~\cite{havet conjectured that the bound on the minimum degree from the previous sentence can be lowered to $\lfloor 2k/3\rfloor$, and this conjecture is supported by evidence found in~\cite{bps, brucemaya1, brucemaya2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-244: The bound $\lfloor 2k/3\rfloor$ would be tight, which can be seen by a small modification of the example from the introduction: Take two copies of the clique on $\lfloor 2k/3\rfloor -1$ vertices and add a universal vertex, and note that the resulting graph does not contain any $k$-edge tree having a vertex with three equal-sized components attached to it.
elements.sent-2411-245: In~\cite{maya-digraphs the following digraph analogue was suggested: \begin{problem [\cite{maya-digraphs ] Determine the smallest $f(k)$ such that for every natural $k$, every digraph of minimum semidegree exceeding $f(k)$ that has a vertex $v$ with $d^+(v),d^-(v)\ge k$ contains each oriented $k$-edge tree.}}}
elements.sent-2411-246: In the forthcoming paper~\cite{giovannegeorgemaya , Kontogeorgiou, Santos and the first author show that asymptotically, $f(k)$ can be taken as $2k/3$ if the tree is large and has bounded degree.}
elements.sent-2411-247: This value would be best possible because of the example from the first paragraph of this section, where we view the host as a digraph and the tree as an oriented tree $T$.
elements.sent-2411-248: Note that $T$ does not need to have large degrees: it can be chosen to have total maximum degree~$3$, and this does not affect the example.
elements.sent-2411-249: For oriented paths, however, the example fails to work.
elements.sent-2411-250: It is possible that for oriented paths we can leave the minimum semidegree bound at $k/2$, and omit the bound on the maximum degree.
elements.sent-2411-251: Indeed, a conjecture from~\cite{survey states that every digraph~$D$ with $\delta^0(D)> k/2$ contains each $k$-edge oriented path.}
elements.sent-2411-252: For progress on this conjecture, see~\cite{antipaths,chen2024, skokanTyomkyn .}
elements.sent-2411-253: We do not know whether in Theorem~\ref{thm:oriented , it is necessary to forbid all orientations of the $4$-cycle.}
elements.sent-2411-254: In our proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:oriented , most of the arguments only require the host $D$ to be \Ccc\ (thus allowing for the presence of directed $4$-cycles).}
elements.sent-2411-255: Perhaps the few places where we relied on forbidding directed $4$-cycles can be handled differently, and then, directed $4$-cycles can be allowed.
elements.sent-2411-256: Possibly also other types of oriented $4$-cycles can be allowed.
elements.sent-2411-258: In the host digraph from this example all four different types of oriented $4$-cycles appear.
elements.sent-2411-259: Interestingly, in the following example only two types of oriented $4$-cycles appear: Take a directed cycle of any length and substitute each vertex with an independent set of $k/2$ vertices.
elements.sent-2411-260: Arcs are inherited from the original directed cycle.
elements.sent-2411-261: It is not hard to see that this oriented graph $O$ fails to contain any antidirected tree whose partition classes have different sizes.
elements.sent-2411-262: The oriented $4$-cycles contained in~$O$ are exactly those that contain no directed path with $3$ arcs.
elements.sent-2411-263: So, the family $\mathcal C_4'$ from Problem~\ref{probo needs to contain at least one of these two oriented cycles.}
elements.sent-2411-264: Another possibility of weakening the conditions on $D$ may be to forbid complete bipartite graphs.
elements.sent-2411-265: In the spirit of~\cite{stein-trujillo , the family $\mathcal K_{2,s}$ of all orientations of $K_{2,s}$ seems a natural candidate.}
elements.sent-2411-266: Dobson~\cite{dobson further conjectured that any graph of girth at least $2\ell + 1$ with $\delta(G) \ge k/\ell$ and $\delta(G)\ge \Delta(T)$ contains each $k$-edge tree, a result confirmed by Jiang~\cite{Jiang01 after prior work by Haxell and {\L uczak~\cite{hax-luc .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-267: Note that the condition $\delta(G)\ge \Delta(T)$ (instead of $\Delta(G)\ge \Delta(T)$) is indeed necessary, which can be seen by considering a balanced double star and a suitable host graph.
elements.sent-2411-268: Graphs with other kinds of expansion properties have also been studied, see for instance~\cite{sudakov .}
elements.sent-2411-269: In the digraph setting, the following question would be a natural analogue of Jiang's result: \begin{question [\cite{maya_LAwomen ] Is it true that every oriented graph $D$ of girth at least $2\ell + 1$ with $\delta^0(D) \ge \max\{k/\ell, \Delta^{tot (T)\}$ contains every $k$-edge oriented tree? \end{question \bibliographystyle{abbrvnat.}}}}}
